segments.s036-g41: خرامان is an adverb/adverbial participle meaning ‘with a proud/stately gait’ and modifies the verb that follows. بیامد is the simple past ‘came’. به نزدیک سرو is a prepositional phrase (به + adjective + noun) meaning ‘toward/close to the cypress’ with به marking direction/position and نزدیک qualifying سرو.
segments.s036-g42: چنان چون is a classical double comparative/concessive pair meaning ‘just as / like’. به پیش گل places the action ‘before/in front of the flower’ (به + پیش + گل). اندر is an archaic preposition meaning ‘in/within, among’, and تذرو is a verbal root meaning ‘to scatter/disperse’ — here it describes the manner invoked by the simile.
segments.s036-g43: زمین را uses را as the definite/object marker marking زمین as the direct object. ببوسید is past tense ‘he kissed’ (classical past). و links clauses (‘and’). چربی نمود uses چربی ‘fat’ as the object/quality and نمود (here past of نمودن) means ‘showed / made apparent’ — ‘he showed/displayed fatness’.
segments.s036-g44: برآن is a locution meaning ‘upon that / about that’. کهتری (from کهتر) refers to smallness/inferiority or modesty; here it qualifies the following noun. آفرین is used as a noun ‘praise / acclamation’. برفزود is past tense ‘he increased / added’ (بر + فزود), so the clause means ‘upon that he increased/added a lesser praise’.
segments.s036-g45: به introduces the indirect object/recipient ‘to Jandal’. جندل here is a proper name (addressed person). چنین is a discourse adverb ‘thus/so’ modifying گفت. گفت in classical contexts is the past of ‘say’ (here ‘said’). شاه یمن is a title phrase ‘the king of Yemen’ (شاه + placename/epithet).
segments.s036-g46: که is the complementizer ‘that’ introducing the quoted wish/claused content. بی‌آفرینت is an adjectival/nominal phrase ‘without praise / unpraised’ referring to the addressee’s mouth. مبادا is an optative/negative jussive particle ‘may it not be / lest’. دهن is ‘mouth’ and is the subject/target of the wish: ‘may your mouth not be unpraised.’
segments.s036-g47: چه پیغام داری is a direct question ‘what message do you have?’ where چه = ‘what’, پیغام = ‘message’, and داری is second-person present ‘you have’. چه فرمان دهی parallels the first question: چه = ‘what’, فرمان = ‘command/order’, دهی is second-person present (classical form of ‘you give’) forming ‘what order do you give?’.
segments.s036-g48: فرستاده‌ای‌گر is a fused conditional: فرستاده ‘sent one / envoy’, ای (copula/2nd-person marker) and گر (classical conditional particle) together yield ‘if you are an envoy/messenger’. گرامی is an epithet ‘honored / esteemed’. رهی (here an adjective/epithet) means ‘of the road / a wayfarer’ or ‘travelling’; together the phrase qualifies the supposed messenger: ‘if you are an honored traveller-messenger’.
segments.s036-g1: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/when' governing سالش ('his year' — سال + enclitic ش). چو here is the temporal 'when'. یک پنجه اندر کشید is a compact poetic phrase: اندر + کشید (past of کشیدن) literally 'drew in one hand‑span'; syntactically it expresses the advance/measurement of his age (i.e. 'his year advanced a hand‑span'). The clause is temporal and sets up the following clause; the verb کشید is past tense and completes the temporal clause.
segments.s036-g2: فرزند‑ش is 'his children' (فرزند + possessive suffix ش). آمد پدید is a two‑word verbal phrase: آمد (came/arrived) + پدید (appeared) used poetically to mean 'came into being' or 'appeared'. گرامی is an adjective 'honoured/dear' modifying فرزند; word order (numeral + noun + possessive + verb) is standard but the light verb phrase آمد پدید gives the birth/appearance sense.
segments.s036-g3: به بخت جهاندار is an adverbial phrase: به (here adverbial 'by') + بخت ('fortune/fate') + جهاندار ('the world‑ruler/sovereign'), meaning roughly 'by the sovereign's fortune' or 'thanks to the ruler's destiny'. هر سه پسر = 'each/ all three sons' — هر before a number gives distributive/emphatic force ('all three'). This phrase sets up the description that follows.
segments.s036-g4: سه خسرو نژاد is a nominal description: سه (three) + خسرو نژاد (a compound meaning 'of kingly/royal stock' — خسرو here 'king/sovereign', نژاد 'lineage'). از در تاج زر is a genitival/locative phrase 'from/of the gate/house of the golden crown' (از = from/of, در = door/gate/threshold, تاج = crown, زر = gold). The whole segment states their royal provenance; word order is noun phrase + clarifying genitive phrase.
segments.s036-g5: The pattern به X چو Y is a poetic simile: به بالا چو سرو = 'in height like a cypress' (به + بالا 'in height', چو 'like', سرو 'cypress/tall beauty'). The conjunction و links the parallel simile به رخ چون بهار = 'and in countenance/face like the spring'. Note the inverted order (به + quality + چو + comparison) typical of poetic diction.
segments.s036-g6: به هر چیز ماننده شهریار is a summarizing phrase: به هر چیز 'in every respect', ماننده (participle/adjective) 'resembling/like', شهریار 'a king/sovereign'. Grammatically, ماننده governs شهریار as the thing they resemble; the whole is an adjectival phrase modifying the sons (implied).
segments.s036-g7: از این سه دو پاکیزه is a truncated clause: 'of these three, two (were) chaste' — the copula/verb is omitted but understood from context. پاکیزه here functions as predicate/adjective 'pure/chaste'. The second از introduces the origin/source: از شهرناز = 'from Shahrnāz' (Shahrnāz is a proper name).
segments.s036-g8: یکی کهتر از خوب‌چهر ارنواز identifies the remaining child: یکی 'one (the other one)', کهتر 'lesser/younger' acting as descriptive adjective, از خوب‌چهر ارنواز 'of fair‑faced Arnawāz' (از = 'from/of', خوب‌چهر = 'fair‑faced', ارنواز a proper name). Again the verb is omitted (understood 'was' or 'came').
segments.s036-g9: This is a compact nominal phrase describing the father: پدر (father) + نوز ناکرده (literally “new/untried” — a compound where ناکرده modifies نوز) + از ناز نام (a prepositional/agentive phrase introduced by از). In older/poetic Persian ناکرده here means “untried/innocent,” and the whole string gives a characterising description (“the father, a newborn/untried one in matters of nâz/fuss and name/reputation”). Word order is noun then adjectival/participial modifiers and then the از‑phrase.
segments.s036-g10: همی is an archaic/poetic preverbal particle giving durative/emphatic coloring; پیلان is the oblique plural of پيل (elephants) used with پیش (in front of). نهادند is the past tense, 3rd person plural of نهادن “to place/set,” and گام is the direct object ‘step’. The literal order is (particle) + preposition + plural noun + verb + object: “they/one continually placed a step before the elephants.”
segments.s036-g11: This phrase names the agent/topic: فریدون (proper name) + از آن نامداران خویش, where از indicates selection/out of, آن = ‘those’, نامداران = ‘renowned ones’, and خویش (here split into خوی + ش) is the possessive meaning ‘his’. The whole means ‘Fereydun, of those famed men of his…’.
segments.s036-g12: یکی را is the marked object (یکی + object marker را). گرانمایه‌تر is a comparative adjective ‘more precious/esteemed’. خواند is the past-tense verb ‘called/named’ (3rd sg past), and پیش here means ‘in the presence of’ or ‘before’. The clause literally: ‘(he) called one (object) the more‑esteemed (adjective) before (him).’
segments.s036-g13: کجا here is an exclamatory/interrogative particle meaning roughly ‘how/where’ used poetically to introduce praise. نام او = ‘his name/reputation’. جندل (a simile noun ‘crag, rock’) followed by پرهنر (‘full of skill/art’) describes that reputation. The phrase links as: ‘How (great) his renown—(it is) a crag, full of skill.’
segments.s036-g14: This is a short adjectival/relational clause: به هر کار = ‘in every matter’, دلسوز = ‘a well‑wisher/compassionate (one)’, بر شاه = ‘toward the king’ (prepositional phrase). The final بر is a reiterative/poetic particle reinforcing direction toward the king. Together: ‘in every affair (he is) a compassionate supporter toward the king.’
segments.s036-g15: بدو گفت is the sequence ‘to him (بدو) said (گفت)’ = ‘he said to him’. برگرد is an imperative/infinitival notion here meaning ‘go around/return through’ and گرد جهان = ‘around the world’ (گرد as ‘around/about’, جهان = ‘world’). The clause functions as the speech act’s verb + direction: ‘He said to him: go around the world...’
segments.s036-g16: This is the command-object: سه دختر گزین = ‘three chosen daughters’ (گزین here adjectival/participial meaning ‘select/chosen’), از نژاد = ‘from the lineage of’, مهان = an epithet for the noble/illustrious line (poetic use). Together: ‘choose three daughters from the noble line.’
segments.s036-g129: سخن‌گفتن is a verbal noun (infinitive) meaning 'speaking' used as a nominal subject; it is coordinated with کوشش by و. آیین ماست is the predicate where آیین 'custom/practice' takes the possessive modifier ما and the copula ست 'is' (poetic form of است). Word order is typical classical Persian: subject(s) + predicate.
segments.s036-g130: عنان و سنان are two nouns joined by و; تافتن is an infinitive/verbal noun that applies to both (literally 'to make shine' or 'to flash'), forming a gerundial phrase that functions as subject of دین ماست ('is our religion'). دین ماست is the copular predicate as above.
segments.s036-g131: به here is instrumental 'by/with'; خنجر is the instrument 'dagger'; زمین را uses را as the definite object marker; کنِیم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative 'let us do / we shall do' (classical poetic usage for a vow or resolution). میستان appears here as the verbal stem combined with کنِیم (poetic compound), so the whole phrase expresses 'with the dagger we will take/seize the earth.'
segments.s036-g132: Parallel structure to the first half: به نیزه 'with the spear' (instrumental), هوا را 'the air' marked as definite object, and کنِیم again marks the optative/vow 'we will do.' نیستان is the verb-stem parallel to میستان in the first hemistich, so the pair means to seize or master 'air' by the spear; word order (instrument + object + verb) is poetic inversion but transparent.
segments.s036-g133: سه فرزند is the noun phrase 'three children/offspring.' اگر begins the conditional 'if'; بر تو هست literally 'is upon you' functions idiomatically as 'you have' (existence with respect to you). ارجمند is a predicate adjective 'esteemed/precious' qualifying the children; classical Persian allows this post-position of the adjective.
segments.s036-g134: سر 'head' is the object of بگشای (an older imperative/present-form meaning 'open, reveal'); بدره 'full moon' is a simile/epithet modifying how the head should be shown ('like the full moon'); و links to the second imperative لب را ببند where را marks لب as the definite direct object and ببند is the imperative 'shut/close.' Word order places the object before the verb, common in Persian poetry.
segments.s036-g135: وگر is the conditional 'if'; چاره کار is a genitival noun phrase 'remedy/way out for the matter' (چاره + کار). خواهی is second-person singular present/future 'you seek/wish' (here 'you seek'); همی is an archaic/emphatic particle used in classical Persian to add emphasis or imperfective aspect ('indeed' / continuous). The whole is 'if you look for a solution.'
segments.s036-g136: بترسی is the 2nd-person form of ترسیدن with the prefix ب- (classical present/subjunctive form used here as future/result in the conditional), meaning 'you will fear'; از indicates the source/object of fear 'of/from'; ین is the orthographic variant of این 'this' attached to پادشاهی 'kingship/kingdom'; همی again is the archaic emphatic/continuous particle. Together: 'you will fear this kingship.'
segments.s036-g49: The clause begins with the dative clitic/adverbial 'بدو' (to him/unto him), followed by the reporting verb 'گفت' (said) and the speaker name 'جندل' (Jandal) — classical Persian often places the indirect object or clitic before the verb. The conjunction 'که' introduces the reported content (’that/…’). Inside the reported clause 'خرم' is an adjective meaning 'happy/joyful' used predicatively and 'بدی' here is ambiguous in form (in the manuscript it looks like the noun 'badī' ‘evil’ but in this position functions as part of the reported predicate — see next segment for sense); the clause as a whole is the beginning of what Jandal says.
segments.s036-g50: This continues the reported speech. 'همیشه' is an adverb 'always'; 'ز' is the poetic/prepositional form of 'from' (here marking separation/source); 'تو' is the second-person pronoun. 'دور' is an adjective/adverb 'far, distant' and 'دست' literally 'hand' (here idiomatically meaning 'reach/control'). The final 'بدی' appears again in the same form as above; syntactically the sequence means ‘you always keep joy far from you and keep harm/evil at a distance from your hand’ — the two halves are parallel, with 'ز تو دور دست' qualifying where/how things are kept.
segments.s036-g51: 'از ایران' is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran'. 'یکی' here is the indefinite 'a certain / someone'. 'کهترم' (an older/poetic form of کهتر) describes that person as 'smaller/younger/lesser' — an epithet meaning ‘a younger or lesser one’. 'چون' introduces a likeness 'like/as', and 'شمن' is a comparative noun 'shaman/sorcerer' — together 'یکی کهترم چون شمن' = 'a certain younger one, like a shaman.' Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase, indefinite subject, modifiers and simile following the noun.
segments.s036-g52: 'پیام آوریده' is a perfect/resultative verbal phrase: 'has brought a message' (آوریده = brought). 'به شاه یمن' is the dative phrase 'to the king of Yemen' (به = to, شاه = king, یمن = Yemen). The whole clause is a classical past/perfect report: someone has brought a message to the king of Yemen.
segments.s036-g53: 'درود ... دهم' is an SOV clause with the object/phrase 'درود' (greeting) and the verb 'دهم' (classical present/subjunctive/optative form of دادن, 'I give' / 'let me give'). 'فریدون فرخ' is the addressee/name plus epithet (Fereydun, 'Farrokh' = fortunate). The order places the object and recipient before the verb, a normal classical Persian ordering; 'دهم' is first-person (‘I give’) and here functions as the speaker's speech act.
segments.s036-g54: This is a conditional/volitional clause: 'سخن هر چه پرسند' literally 'the speech/words — whatever they ask' (هر چه + پرسند = 'whatever they ask' with پرسند a classical third-person (plural) subjunctive/indicative used in reported/general statements). 'پاسخ دهم' contains the verb 'دهم' again ('I give/ I will give') with 'پاسخ' as object, yielding 'I will answer' / 'I give an answer.' The structure expresses willingness to answer any question ('whatever they ask, I will answer').
segments.s036-g55: The phrase 'ترا آفرین' is an archaic/poetic expression meaning 'praise to you' (ترا = to you, formed from the old object clitic 'ت' + 'را'); here 'آفرین' is a noun/exclamation 'praise' or 'bravo'. 'از فریدون گرد' literally 'from Fereydun’s circle/assembly' — 'از' = from, 'فریدون' the name, and 'گرد' here denotes a group/round/assembly (poetic use). The whole short clause is a formulaic praise 'Praise to you from Fereydun’s circle.'
segments.s036-g56: This is a copular/exclamatory clause: 'بزرگ' (great) is predicate/adjective placed before the subject 'آن‌کسی' (that person/the one who). 'کو' here is the archaic relative particle 'who/which' introducing the relative clause, and 'نداردش' is a compound verb 'does not have it/him' where the enclitic '-ش' refers back to 'خرد' (wisdom). 'خرد' is 'wisdom'. So the literal order is 'Great is that one who has not wisdom' — i.e., 'Great is the one who lacks wisdom' (likely ironic or proverbial).
segments.s036-g17: سه خواهر is the noun phrase 'three sisters' (numeral before noun). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' indicating parentage; ز یک مادر و یک پدر = 'of one mother and one father' (the possessor follows the possessed noun as in classical Persian).
segments.s036-g18: A string of descriptive elements without a copula: پری چهره 'fairy-faced' (پری modifies چهره), پاک 'pure' and then the noun pair خسرو گهر functioning as an epithet ('king/royal' + 'gem') — a poetic noun-noun epithet meaning something like 'jewel fit for a king'. Adjectives/epithets follow or juxtapose the head noun in this poetic construction.
segments.s036-g19: به خوبی here qualifies 'سزای' (سزا = 'desert/recompense') — literally 'the good recompense'; سه فرزند من is a genitive phrase 'my three children' with the possessor من following the noun, so the whole means 'a good recompense for my three children.'
segments.s036-g20: چنان چون is a redundant/ emphatic 'such as' or 'in such a way as' linking to the previous clause. بشاید is a literary third-person present/optative of شایستن meaning 'it is fitting' or 'it ought to be.' به پیوند من = 'for/to my bond' (پیوند = bond, link) — the preposition به introduces the noun, with من as possessor.
segments.s036-g21: به بالا و دیدار is a prepositional phrase 'in rank/standing and in sight/appearance' (به + noun). هر سه یکی literally 'each/ all three one' = 'the three are the same' — Persian often places the quantifier and determiner before the numeral (هر سه) and then states 'one' to mean 'identical/as one.'
segments.s036-g22: که introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause. این را (را = definite object marker) is the object 'this'; ندانند is the negative present form 'they do not know' (or 'are unaware'). ازان اندکی is an ablative/partitive phrase 'of those, a few' = 'only a few (of them)': the order places the verb before the limiting phrase for emphasis.
segments.s036-g23: چو is the temporal 'when.' بشنید is the past third-person 'heard.' جندل here functions as the subject (given the senses listed, treated as 'a small troop/group' rather than a proper name). ز خسرو سخن = 'word/speech of Khosrow' with ز = 'about/from' indicating source or topic.
segments.s036-g24: یکی رای پاکیزه is the object phrase 'one pure counsel/plan' (یکی = one, رای = counsel/plan, پاکیزه = pure/clean as an attributive). افگند is the past 'he cast/put forth' (literary use meaning 'devised' or 'proposed'); بن (literally 'root'/'basis') is an adverbial/locative adding the sense 'at the root' or 'fundamentally' — here idiomatically indicating that he put forward a plan from its foundation.
segments.s036-g25: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' linking this descriptive clause to the preceding noun. The clause bīdār del bud is an adjectival phrase + past copula (adjective بیدار 'alert' + noun دل 'heart' + بود 'was' in the past). The coordinate phrase و پاکیزه مغز follows the same pattern (پاکیزه modifies مغز); the past copula is already supplied by بود earlier and governs the whole coordinated description.
segments.s036-g26: This is a nominal description: زبان is the head 'tongue/speech', followed by adjectives and adjectival phrases. چرب here is adjectival = 'flattering/smooth-tongued'. The coordinate شایسته کار نغز is a compressed phrase: شایسته ('fitting') modifies the noun phrase کار نغز ('a fine/pithy work' — i.e. skilled/pithy expression). Word order places the head (زبان) before its qualifiers.
segments.s036-g27: ز پیش is a locative phrase 'from/in front of', placed before the noun it modifies (سپهبد). برون شد is a verb complex where the adverbial برون 'out/forth' combines with شد (past of شدن) to mean 'went out' or 'left'. به راه is the directional prepositional phrase 'onto the road/ on his way' and follows the verb phrase.
segments.s036-g28: ابا here expresses reluctance or refusal (a verbal/adjectival idea 'hesitating/reluctant'). چند تن is a quantified noun phrase 'a few persons'. The sequence مر ورا uses archaic enclitic pronouns (مر = 'me', ورا = 'to him/thither') and is idiomatic; نیکخواه qualitatively modifies those persons as 'well-wishers'. Together the phrase describes accompaniment by a few well‑wishers while showing some reluctance.
segments.s036-g29: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' or 'each in turn' and precedes the rest of the clause. ز ایران is a genitive/relational phrase 'from/of Iran' qualifying who is being summoned. سراندر is a locative/illative expression 'within/into (his) presence', and کشید is the past verb 'drew/dragged/summoned' (classical usage allows 'کشیدن' with the sense of bringing or calling someone). The verb appears at the clause end, typical of Persian word order.
segments.s036-g30: پژوهید is a past tense verb meaning 'they examined/ questioned/ inquired' (here describing the active questioning). The coordination و links to the following nominal/verb pair; هرگونه modifies گفت و شنید as 'every kind of saying and hearing' — an idiomatic expression meaning 'every sort of report or account'. شنید is the past tense 'they heard' (completing the sequence 'questioned and heard').
segments.s036-g31: به هر کشوری is a locative phrase 'in/for every country/realm' (به + هر + noun). The relative particle کز ('which/that') introduces a restrictive relative clause. جهان مهتری is a classical genitival/attributive phrase meaning 'preeminent in the world' or 'the greatest in the world' (مهتری modifying وطن/جهان in the sense 'foremost').
segments.s036-g32: به پرده درون is a locative phrase 'inside/behind the curtain' (preposition + place + directional/inward adverb). داشتن here is the verb 'to have / to keep' used statively to mean 'there was / they had kept', and دختری is its object 'a maiden' (indefinite). The clause thus states possession/existence of a veiled maiden in each such realm.
segments.s036-g121: ازین در سخن = a prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'on this matter/topic' (از + این with poetic spelling 'ازین', followed by در 'in/topic' and سخن 'speech/topic'). هر چه دارید یاد is a relative clause in inverted poetic order: هر چه دارید 'whatever you have/possess (i.e. whatever words you have)', with یاد (a noun 'remembrance/recollection') placed after it to mean 'what you recall'. داريد is present 2nd‑person plural/polite 'you have'.
segments.s036-g122: سراسر 'entirely/throughout' qualifies the following clause. به من 'to me' marks the beneficiary/recipient. بر + بباید + گشاد is a verbal phrase: بباید (must/ought) + گشاد (past participle/adjective 'open') with the prefix/preposition بر giving the sense 'be opened to' — together meaning 'it must be opened to me' or 'you must open it up to me.' Word order is poetic (adverbial + indirect object + modal + participle).
segments.s036-g123: جهان‌آزموده دلاور is an attributive phrase: جهان آزموده 'world‑tested/experienced' + دلاور 'brave' together modify the head noun سران 'chiefs/heads'. Adjectival epithets precede the noun in this poetic sequence.
segments.s036-g124: گشادند is a 3rd‑person plural verbal/adjectival form from گشاد (here 'opened'), agreeing with سران; in context it conveys 'they opened' (i.e. opened their mouths). یک‌یک 'one by one' is an adverbial clarifying manner. به پاسخ زبان is an idiom 'to answer with the tongue' (به = towards/for, پاسخ = answer, زبان = speech/tongue), i.e. 'to reply verbally.'
segments.s036-g125: که introduces the clause 'that/for'. ما همگنان آن is 'we, his peers/equals' with آن pointing back to the subject referred to; word order is inverted for emphasis. نبینیم رای uses نبینیم (1st‑person plural negative of دیدن, 'we do not see/consider') with رای 'counsel/opinion' as the object (poetic order). Together: 'we do not see/accept that counsel.'
segments.s036-g126: که again links the next thought ('for/that'). هر باد را 'every wind/breeze' is topicalized with the object marker را; تو بجنبی ز جای is a subordinate clause meaning 'you are moved/stirred from your place' — بجنبی is 2nd‑person (present/subjunctive/poetic) of جنبیدن 'to move' used intransitively/passively here, ز جای 'from (your) place.' The sense: 'you are swayed by every breeze.'
segments.s036-g127: اگر شد = 'if (he) became' (اگر introducing the conditional; شد is past/ perfective 'became'). فریدون is the subject (proper name/legendary king). جهان شهریار is a genitive-like phrase 'ruler/king of the world' (noun + title), so the whole clause reads 'if Fereydun became king of the world.'
segments.s036-g128: نه at the start negates what follows: 'no / not so.' ما بندگانیم is 'we are servants' (بندگانیم = we are servants/subjects); with نه it means 'we are not servants.' با گوشوار is a prepositional phrase 'with earrings/ornaments' used metaphorically to mean 'mere ornaments' — 'we are not slaves to be adorned with earrings.'
segments.s036-g65: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' used here in the idiom meaning 'free of' (i.e. not needing). The phrase 'هر کام و هر خواسته' lists two synonymous objects ('every desire and every wish'); بی‌نیاز is a predicative adjective meaning 'needing nothing / self-sufficient' and functions as the clause's predicate with an omitted copula (typical in poetry).
segments.s036-g66: به هر آرزو is a prepositional phrase 'toward/to every wish'; دست ایشان is a possessive nominal phrase 'their hand' (دست + ایشان). دراز here is a postpositive predicate/adjective meaning 'extended' or 'stretched (out)'; Persian often omits a copula, so the sense is 'their hand is extended toward every wish.'
segments.s036-g67: مر is an archaic/pronoun element ('me' / 'to me') appearing in older Persian; here it participates in a clause that places the three precious items as definite objects ('این سه گرانمایه را'). در نهفت is a prepositional/locative expression 'in hiding' or 'hidden inside' with نهفت used as a participial/adjectival form 'hidden, concealed'; را marks the definite object.
segments.s036-g68: بباید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / must' (here functioning as a modal verb). کنون is the adverb 'now'; the nominal sequence 'شاهزاده سه جفت' ('three pairs of princes') is the thing required by that necessity. Word order is somewhat poetic (modal + adverb + noun phrase).
segments.s036-g69: ز introduces a source ('from'); کار here means 'affair' or 'matter'. آگهان (here acting adverbially) means 'suddenly' and modifies the verb that follows. آگهی is the noun 'news/information' and یافتم is the past-tense first-person verb 'I found/obtained' (so: 'I suddenly obtained news of the affair').
segments.s036-g70: بدین (here written as the prefix ب + دین) functions as 'with this / at this' — the prefix ب- attaches to indicate 'by/with', forming 'بدین آگهی' = 'with this news'. تیز is an adverb/adjective 'swift/quick', and بشتافتم is the past tense 'I hastened/ran' (first-person). Together: 'At this news I hurried swiftly.'
segments.s036-g71: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. از پس literally 'from after' here functions as 'from behind / beyond' and combines with پرده 'curtain/veil' to give 'behind the veil'. پوشیده is an adjective/participle 'hidden/covered' modifying روی 'face(s)'; the overall phrase asks 'where (are) the faces hidden behind the veil?'.
segments.s036-g72: سه پاکیزه is the noun phrase 'the three chaste/clean ones' (پاکیزه here has the sense 'chaste/pure' in this poetic context). داری is the second-person present 'you have / you keep' (asking where you keep them). تو‌ای is a vocative 'you, O', and نامجوی is a vocative epithet 'name-seeker' (one seeking fame), addressing the addressee.
segments.s036-g81: فریدون is the named subject (the legendary king). پیامم is پیام + ـم (my message). بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning “in this manner” (بدین = به + این, archaic/poetic form), and داد is the simple past ‘gave.’ Word order is somewhat topicalized: the giver and the message are presented before the verb.
segments.s036-g82: تو is the vocative/subject 'you.' پاسخ گزار is an imperative/infinitival command construction: پاسخ (answer) + گزار (here used as a verb 'render/report/give') — an archaic poetic usage meaning 'make/offer an answer.' آنچه is a relative/pronominal compound 'that which/whatever.' آیدت is آید + ـت 'comes to you' (present/future sense), and یاد here means 'memory/what comes to mind.'
segments.s036-g83: پیامش is پیام + ـش 'his/its message.' چو + بشنید is a temporal clause: چو = 'when' and بشنید is past tense 'he heard.' شاه یمن is the nominal subject 'the king of Yemen' (Yemen here = place/name), placed after the temporal clause in poetic word order.
segments.s036-g84: بپژمرد is a past tense verb meaning 'he withered/wilted' (ب- is the verbal prefix). چون here = 'like/as.' زاب is the river-name Zab (used as the agent of uprooting), کنده is the participial adjective 'uprooted/torn up,' and سمن is 'jasmine' (the simile’s figure). The simile reads 'he withered like jasmine uprooted by the Zab.'
segments.s036-g85: همی‌گفت‌گر is a progressive/iterative past form 'kept saying' or 'was saying' (همی- marks ongoing/repeated action; the -گر ending is archaic/poetic). پیش بالین من means 'before/by my bedside' (پیش = before/in the presence of; بالین = 'pillow' → 'bedside'), with من marking the possessor/experiencer 'my.'
segments.s036-g86: نبیند is the negative form of دیدن (ن + بیند) used here to express 'will not see' / 'does not see' in the reported speech. سه ماه is a temporal accusative 'three months' (time span after the verb). این جهان‌بین من ('this world-seer of mine') is the topicalized noun phrase that names who will not see that period; جهان‌بین is an epithet 'world-wise/world-seer' and من is the possessive 'my.'
segments.s036-g87: مرا is the experiencer/dative 'to me' (here marking the person affected), followed by a verbless or copular predication 'روز روشن بود' = 'the day was bright.' This is a topicalized structure common in poetry: 'To me the day was bright.' تاره شب is a noun phrase 'the new/renewed night' (تاره = new/anew modifying شب).
segments.s036-g88: بباید is an impersonal/modal construction 'it is necessary / one must.' گشادن is the infinitive 'to open' (the action required). به here marks purpose or direction 'for/in (order) to,' and پاسخ دو لب literally 'answer of two lips' means 'to open the two lips in answer' (دو لب = 'two lips' = 'my lips'). The phrase thus expresses necessity: 'my two lips must open to answer.'
segments.s036-g73: This noun phrase names what was heard. 'مران' is a proper name (here the name heard). 'هرسه' ('all three') follows the name as a clarifying numeral phrase. The clitic 'را' marks the phrase as definite/accusative. 'نوز' (new/fresh) plus the negated participle 'ناکرده' form an adjectival phrase modifying 'نام' — literally 'a name not yet made/uttered', i.e. a fresh/unspoken name.
segments.s036-g74: 'چو بشنیدم' is a temporal clause meaning 'when I heard' (چو = when; بشنیدم = I heard). The main clause that follows is verbless in appearance but uses 'شدم' (I became) as the finite verb: 'این دل شدم شادکام' literally 'this heart I became fortunate' — 'دل' is the (logical) subject/experiencer and 'شادکام' is a predicate adjective meaning 'glad/fortunate'. Word order is emphatic/poetic (object/subject precedes the verb).
segments.s036-g75: 'که' introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause 'that / for'. 'ما نیز' = 'we also.' 'نام' (name) is the direct theme of the clause. 'سه فرخ نژاد' is a noun phrase 'the three of fortunate/auspicious race' (فرخ = fortunate; نژاد = lineage/lineage). Together this phrase sets up what was not recalled.
segments.s036-g76: Temporal clause headed by 'چو' = 'when'. 'اندر خور آید' is an idiomatic locution meaning 'comes into the sun / comes to light' (اندر = in/into; خور = sun), so 'when it came to light'. 'نکردیم' is the negative past 'we did not do' taking the following noun 'یاد' (remembrance) as its object: 'we did not remember.' The verb-object order ('نکردیم یاد') is an archaic/poetic collocation meaning 'did not remember'.
segments.s036-g77: 'کنون' (now) is a fronted adverb. 'این گرامی دو گونه گهر' is the noun phrase 'these precious (گرامی) two kinds (دو گونه) of gems (گهر)' — 'گرامی' here functions as 'precious/valued' modifying the following compound; the whole phrase names the items to be combined.
segments.s036-g78: 'بباید' is an impersonal modal 'it is necessary / must'. 'برآمیخت' is a perfect/past stem used in a passive/necessitative sense 'be mingled / be combined'; together they convey 'must be mingled'. 'با یکدگر' is the prepositional phrase 'with one another' indicating the parties to be mixed. The construction is typical Persian modal + participle, giving obligation to a stative result.
segments.s036-g79: This phrase lists two coordinated noun groups. 'سه پوشیده رخ را' — 'the three veiled faces' — uses 'را' as the definite/accusative marker attached to the first noun phrase (poetic topicalization). 'سه دیهیم جوی' follows as a parallel phrase: 'دیهیم' (rural, villager) modifies 'جوی' (seeker/searcher), so 'the three rural seekers.' The two groups are presented in apposition or parallel.
segments.s036-g80: This is a verbless predication common in Persian poetry: 'سزا را سزاوار' — literally 'the due (سزا) is deserving (سزاوار)' — where 'سزا' (their due/desert) is marked by 'را' and 'سزاوار' is the predicate adjective 'worthy/deserving'. 'بی‌گفت‌وگوی' is an adverbial compound 'without speaking / without debate', modifying the predicate; the line thus states that they are deserving of their due without argument. The copula 'است' is omitted (elliptical).
segments.s036-g137: ازو is the prepositional pronoun 'from him/from it' placed before the noun phrase to mark source. آرزو‌های is the plural 'wishes/desires' (here the possessed/object of seeking). پرمایه جوی is a compound modifier: پرمایه 'substantial' + جوی 'seeking/searching', so together they describe the kind of desires (literally 'seeking substantial things' → 'weighty/substantial desires'). The whole phrase means 'those desires sought from him' (poetic word order places ازو up front).
segments.s036-g138: که introduces a relative clause 'which/who'. کردار is 'deed, action' and آنرا is the demonstrative object 'that (it/that thing)'; together کردار آنرا = 'the deed of that / that deed'. نبینند is a third-person plural present-subjunctive/negative form meaning 'they do not see / may not see' (classical Persian uses this form in relative clauses like this). روی here means 'face' (i.e. 'no face sets eyes on it' — a poetic way to say 'unseen').
segments.s036-g139: چو is the conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past tense verb 'he/they heard' (classical past). از marks the source/concerned-about ('of/about'), آن 'that' points to the thing previously mentioned, نامداران is the plural noun 'the renowned/famous ones' used as the agents, and سخن is 'speech/talk/news'. Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal conjunction + past verb + prepositional phrase giving what was heard.
segments.s036-g140: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. سر here means 'the high/top' (metonymically 'the high/ruling or foremost'), دید is the simple past 'saw', آن را is the accusative demonstrative 'it' (the thing referred to), به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' (به used prepositionally with گیتی 'world/earth'), and بن is 'root/base' (the counterpart to سر). The clause says that neither high nor low (neither summit nor foundation) in the world had seen it.
segments.s036-g105: همی‌کرد is an archaic/perfective-continuous auxiliary (here indicating an action about to occur or habitual/continuous aspect). خواهد is a modal/future marker meaning 'will/is going to' so combined they give the sense 'was about to / would'. ز + چشمم جدا is a prepositional phrase: ز (poetic 'from') + چشم (eye/sight) + -م (enclitic 'my'), and جدا 'apart' — together: 'to be separated from my sight' (i.e. to leave my view).
segments.s036-g106: یکی رای باید زدن با شما is a compact clause meaning 'we must strike/come up with a single plan with you.' یکی 'one / a certain' modifies رای (here 'plan, counsel'), باید is the necessity/modal 'must/it is necessary', زدن is the infinitive/compound verb used idiomatically with رای (literally 'to strike/give a counsel' = 'to propose/decide'), and با شما 'with you' marks the partner.
segments.s036-g107: فرستاده گوید چنین گفت شاه is reported speech: فرستاده 'the messenger' is subject, گوید is the (historical/present) reporting verb 'says/said (reports)', then چنين گفت شاه is the quoted content reversed in poetic word order: چنين 'thus', گفت (past 'said') + شاه 'the king' — i.e. 'the messenger says that the king said thus.'
segments.s036-g108: که ما را سه شاه‌ست زیبای گاه is a subordinate clause introduced by که 'that'. ما را uses را as a topical/ethical marker ('for us' / 'we have'), سه 'three' modifies شاه‌ست (شاه + است fused: 'is/are king(s)'), and زیبای گاه is an adjectival phrase 'beautiful (زیبای) of aspect/time (گاه)' — together: 'that we have three kings, fair of aspect.'
segments.s036-g109: گراینده is an agent-noun (present-participle-like) from گراییدن meaning 'the one who inclines/turns/brings'. هر سه means 'each/whichever of the three' (هر modifying سه). به پیوند من is a prepositional phrase 'to my bond/union/alliance' (به 'to' + پیوند 'bond' + من 'my' as possessor). The clause thus expresses 'the one who brings any of the three to my alliance.'
segments.s036-g110: به سه روی پوشیده فرزند من: به 'with' or 'in' + سه روی 'three-faced / three sides' (روی = face/side), پوشیده 'hidden/concealed' qualifies فرزند من 'my child/my son' (فرزند + enclitic/possessor من). The phrase describes the son as 'concealed/covered with three faces.'
segments.s036-g111: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. گویم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I say' (here used in the protasis). آری is the simple interjection 'yes'. و 'and' links the next clause: دل 'the heart' + زان 'of that / from that' + تهی 'empty' — 'and the heart is empty of that.'
segments.s036-g112: دروغم نه اندر خورد با مهی: دروغم 'my lie' (دروغ + enclitic -م). نه اندر خورد is a negated verbal phrase meaning 'does not go down / is not taken / is not acceptable' (اندر خورد used idiomatically as 'to be received/accepted'). با مهی 'with the moon‑like one' (مهی = moon‑like/beautiful person) indicates the party for whom the lie would not be acceptable.
segments.s036-g33: نهفته is an adjective/participle 'hidden' placed before the verb to qualify what was sought; بجستی is the simple past, 2nd-person singular of بجستن ('you sought/searched'). همه راز‌شان is the direct object phrase 'all their secrets' (راز + enclitic possessive شان). Word order (Adjective + Verb + Object) is poetic but means 'you sought all their hidden secrets.'
segments.s036-g34: شنیدی is the simple past, 2nd-person singular of شنیدن ('you heard'); همه نام و آواز‌شان is the coordinated object 'all their names and their voices/songs' (نام and آواز joined by و, with the possessive enclitic شان attached to آواز). The verb applies to the whole coordinated noun phrase.
segments.s036-g35: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/among'; دهقان here means the landed gentry/dihqān (a class of wealthy landholders) and پر مایه is a compound describing them as 'rich/ample in resources'. کس را is the object (کس used under a negated verb means 'anyone/no one') with را the definite/object marker; ندید is 3rd-person singular past 'he did not see.' Together: 'He saw no one among the wealthy landowners.'
segments.s036-g36: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; پیوسته is an adverb 'constantly/ever'; آفریدون is the proper name Afrīdūn; سزید is the past form of سزیدن ('to befit; to deserve') used here to mean 'was worthy/fit.' The clause modifies کس: 'who was ever worthy of Afrīdūn' (i.e. who could stand in Afrīdūn's place or befit Afrīdūn).
segments.s036-g37: A string of adjectives joined by و (خردمند و روشن‌دل و پاک‌تن) precedes the verb and describes the (implied) subject. This adjectival series is nominative/predicative and functions as the subject phrase in classical poetic order ('wise and clear‑hearted and pure‑bodied').
segments.s036-g38: بیامد is 3rd-person singular past 'he came.' بر is the preposition meaning 'to/toward' here; سرو ('cypress') is a metaphorical epithet often used for a tall/beautiful person and here stands with شاه یمن as a genitive construction 'the cypress of the king of Yemen.' Classical poetry commonly omits the ezāfe vowel, so the juxtaposition سرو شاه یمن reads as 'the cypress of the king of Yemen.'
segments.s036-g39: نشان یافت is '(he) found a sign' (نشان = sign/token, یافت = 3rd-person past 'found'). جندل is a locative/setting 'on the crag/rock' (classical poetic word order allows the place to appear after the verb). مر is the enclitic/object 'to me' and اورا is the object pronoun 'him/her/them' — both follow the place phrase here as objects of یافت; درست is an adverb/adjective 'proper/exact' modifying the finding. The whole phrase reads as 'he found a sign on the crag, exactly/rightly for me and for her.'
segments.s036-g40: سه دختر is the object 'three daughters.' چنان چون is the fixed correlative 'just as / in the same way as'; فریدون is the proper name Fereydun; بجست is 3rd-person past of بجستن ('he sought/searched/found'). Together: 'he sought three daughters, just as Fereydun had sought.' Note the poetic inversion and economy of particles typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s036-g57: مرا گفت is an instance of reported speech where مرا (= “me”, object) + گفت (“he said/told”) introduces what was said. شاه یمن را بگوی is an imperative/instructive clause: بگوی is the verb 'tell' (2nd singular imperative/optative form here) and یمن را is the direct object marked by را (‘the king of Yemen’). Word order is typical Persian: direct object (شاه یمن را) before the verb (بگوی).
segments.s036-g58: که introduces the content of the instruction (‘that …’). بر گاه is a fixed locution meaning ‘at the appointed time / when the hour comes’ (بر + گاه = on/time). تا here functions as a temporal/subordinating particle ‘when/until’ linking to مشک بوید (بوید is an Old Persian/poetic present-subjunctive form of ‘to smell/be fragrant’). ببوی is the imperative ‘smell!’ (second-person), directed to the addressee. The clause therefore gives the instruction: at the appointed time, when the musk gives scent, smell it.
segments.s036-g59: بدان‌ای is the vocative/imperative phrase ‘know this, O …’ (بدان = ‘know’, with -ای as a vocative particle). سر مایه is a compound meaning ‘head/prime resource’ or ‘chief treasure’; تازیان is the genitive/possessive complement ‘of the Arabs’ (vocative address). The phrase addresses someone directly: “Know, O chief/treasure of the Arabs.”
segments.s036-g60: کز = 'که + ز' (classical contraction) here functions as a subordinating particle meaning ‘that/from which’. اختر بدی is a genitival phrase ‘star of evil’ (اختر = star, بدی = evil/harm). جاودان بی‌زیان is a predicate/adjectival phrase meaning ‘everlasting, without harm’ (جاودان = eternal; بی‌زیان = harmless). The clause states the asserted fact introduced by بدان‌ای.
segments.s036-g61: مرا پادشاهی آباد هست is a verbless/existential construction where مرا (literally ‘to me’) marks the experiencer/possessor: ‘to me there is a prosperous kingship’ → ‘I have a prosperous kingship’. آباد is an adjective modifying پادشاهی, and هست is the copula ‘is/exists’ placed at the end (typical Persian word order).
segments.s036-g62: همان introduces a pointing/identifying noun phrase (‘that very…’). گنج و مردی و نیروی دست is a list of nouns linked by و (‘and’): ‘treasure (گنج), manliness/valour (مردی), and the strength/power of the hand (نیروی دست).’ Note the nominal coordination and the genitival compound نیرویِ دست = ‘force/strength of the hand’.
segments.s036-g63: سه فرزند شایسته تاج و گاه is a noun phrase listing quantity + noun + adjective: سه فرزند = ‘three children/sons’, شایسته = ‘worthy’, followed by تاج و گاه = ‘of the crown and of the occasion/time’ (گاه here carries the sense ‘time/occasion’). Word order is numeral → noun → adjective → additional modifiers.
segments.s036-g64: اگر introduces a conditional ‘if’. داستان را بود is a classical phrasing where را marks داستان as topical/definite and بود is past copula ‘was/were’ (here functioning like ‘there were/was’). گاه ماه combines two nouns: گاه (‘time/occasion’) and ماه (‘moon’ or poetically ‘the beautiful one’) — together conveying ‘the moon’s hour’ or ‘the time of the moon’ (a poetic way to mark a notable moment). The clause thus reads: ‘if in the tale there was a time, (it was) the moon’s time.’
segments.s036-g89: سراینده را گفت is a topicalized construction: سراینده (the poet/singer) is marked by را and functions as the addressee of گفت (said). In classical Persian poetry را can mark the person spoken to rather than a direct object. کای is the vocative 'O' addressing the following name (نامجوی).
segments.s036-g90: زمان باید is an impersonal/modal statement: باید expresses necessity ('must/ought'), with زمان as the thing whose proper timing is being referred to; اندر such گفت‌گوی is a locative/prepositional phrase ('in/within such a conversation'). اندر is an archaic preposition for 'in'. The clause is somewhat elliptical (the rest of the obligation is supplied by the surrounding lines).
segments.s036-g91: شتابت (your haste) carries the second-person suffix and is topicalized at the front. نباید is the negative modal 'should/must not'; it follows the topic here. به پاسخ is a prepositional phrase 'in/for answering' (به + noun), and کنون is the adverb 'now' placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s036-g92: مرا is the dative/possessive 'to me' (marking possession in older Persian idiom). راز‌ست is the compound of راز + -ست (copula 'is'), so the sequence means 'there is/are secrets to me' (= 'I have secrets'). چند quantifies those secrets ('several/a few'), and با رهنمون means 'with a guide' or 'with guidance'. Note the classical word-order: the copula is attached to راز (رازست) rather than standing alone.
segments.s036-g93: فرستاده را marks 'the messenger' (را as object/recipient marker) and گزید is the past tense 'he chose'. زود (quickly/soon) modifies گزید and جایی ('a place') is the object chosen; the sequence places the adverb and place between object and verb in a poetic order.
segments.s036-g94: پس آنگه are consecutive temporal adverbs ('then' / 'afterwards') used redundantly for emphasis. به کار اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'into the inward matter/affair' (اندرون = inner quarters/inside), and بنگرید (classical form with the b- prefix) is a past/narrative perfect of 'to look' meaning 'he looked into' or 'he examined'.
segments.s036-g95: بیامد is the past 'he came'. در بار here means 'into the court/audience' (بار in classical usage = an occasion/presence before the ruler). دادن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to give' (i.e., to present/give his report or gift) and ببست is the past 'he closed/shut' (likely the doors or the proceedings). The three verbs form a short sequence of actions.
segments.s036-g96: به انبوه اندیشگان در نشست is a locative phrase: به marks location/setting, انبوه qualifies اندیشگان ('a throng/multitude of the thoughtful/wise'), and در نشست specifies that they were 'in the session/assembly'. The whole phrase describes many wise men seated at the gathering.
segments.s036-g97: فراوان کس از دشت نیزه‌وران is a noun phrase meaning 'many people/soldiers from the plain who are spearmen.' Word order here is determiner/adverb (فراوان) + head noun (کس) + origin phrase (از دشت) + descriptive plural compound (نیزه‌وران). فراوان modifies کس; از introduces provenance ('from the plain'); نیزه‌وران is a compound noun 'spearmen' describing those people.
segments.s036-g98: بر خویش خواند آزموده سران is a clause: بر خویش 'to/unto his own side' (بر = toward/to; خویش = خوی + enclitic ش = 'his own'), خواند is the narrative past verb used as 'he called/summoned' (here sense 'to call/summon'), and آزموده سران is the object phrase 'the tried/experienced leaders' (آزموده = 'proven/experienced' modifying سران = 'leaders/heads'). Note that خویش is formed from خوی + ش (enclitic possessive).
segments.s036-g99: نهفته برون آورید از نهفت is a verb phrase meaning 'brought forth the hidden from concealment.' نهفته = 'the hidden' (adjective/participle used nominally), برون آورید = 'brought out' (آورید here functions narratively as a past/preterite action in epic language or as a perfective 'brought out'), and از نهفت = 'from concealment' (از = 'from'; نهفت = 'hidden/secret place'). Word order places the thing revealed (نهفته) before the verb.
segments.s036-g100: همه راز‌ها پیش ایشان بگفت is 'they/one told all the secrets before them.' همه راز‌ها = 'all the secrets' (Raz‑ha is plural with the plural marker -ها), پیش ایشان = 'in their presence / before them' (پیش = 'before / in front of / in the presence of'; ایشان = 'them'), and بگفت is the narrative past 'said/told.' The clause therefore reports that the secrets were spoken in front of those people.
segments.s036-g101: که ما را به گیتی ز پیوند خویش is a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that'). ما را = 'to/for us' (را is the object/definiteness marker attached to ما), به گیتی = 'in the world' (به functioning as a locative/relational preposition here), ز پیوند خویش = 'from/by the bond of his kin' (ز = poetic 'from/of'; پیوند = 'bond/link'; خویش = 'his own' formed from خوی + enclitic ش). The phrase gives the beneficiary and cause/background for what follows.
segments.s036-g102: سه شمع‌ست روشن به دیدار پیش is the main predication: سه شمع‌ست = 'three candles are' (شمع‌ست is a fused copular/nominal form), روشن = 'bright/illuminated' (predicate adjective), and به دیدار پیش = 'before the (one's) sight / in sight' (به دیدار = 'to/for sight'; پیش = 'before/in front of'). The copular construction places the numeral phrase as subject and روشن as predicative description.
segments.s036-g103: فریدون فرستاد زی من پیام is a simple past clause: فریدون = proper name (subject), فرستاد = 'sent' (past tense, third person singular), زی من پیام = 'a message to me' (زی here is an archaic preposition used with من to indicate the recipient; پیام = 'message'). In older epic Persian, such prepositional forms (زی) occur instead of the modern را/به combinations.
segments.s036-g104: بگسترد پیشم یکی خوب دام is 'he spread before me a fine trap.' بگسترد = narrative past 'he spread/extended' (ب- prefix marks narrative), پیشم = 'before me / in my presence' (پیش + ـم enclitic 'me'), یکی = 'one / a certain' (here indefinite 'a'), خوب = 'good/skilful/fine' modifying دام = 'trap.' Word order is verb (بگسترد) then locative (پیشم) then object phrase (یکی خوب دام).
segments.s036-g113: وگر is a conditional conjunction ('if' / 'but if'). آرزو‌ها is the plural definite/indefinite noun 'wishes/desires'. سپارم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive or optative of سپردن ('I entrust' / 'I would entrust'); in a conditional clause it expresses a hypothetical action. بدوی is a poetic/archaic dative/clitic form meaning 'to you' / 'to him' (contracted from به + او/بدو) and functions as the indirect object of سپارم.
segments.s036-g114: شود is third‑person singular of شدن used here as a potential/epic result ('becomes' / 'would become'). دل is the grammatical subject 'heart'. پر + noun ('پر آتش') is a predicate phrase meaning 'full (of) fire' (پر = 'full (of)'). The second پر governs the following phrase ('پر از آب روی') meaning 'and (the) face full of water' (پر از = 'full of'; روی = 'face'); word order is poetic, so two 'full of' predicates apply to heart and face respectively.
segments.s036-g115: وگر = 'if'. سر بپیچم is an idiomatic verbal phrase: سر (head) + بپیچم (first‑person present/subjunctive of پیچیدن, 'I turn/turn away') meaning 'I turn my head away' or 'I disobey.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from/away from'. فرمان او = 'his command' (او is the possessive/3rd‑person pronoun). The clause expresses a hypothetical deviation from obedience.
segments.s036-g116: به یک‌سو is an adverbial phrase 'to one side / aside' (به = toward; یک‌سو = one side). گرایم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of the archaic/poetic verb گرا(یدن) 'to incline / turn' and here means 'I would turn/ I incline.' ز پیمان او = 'from his covenant' (ز = from; پیمان او = his pledge). The whole clause restates the hypothetical deviation in other words.
segments.s036-g117: کسی = 'someone' / 'one'. کو here is an archaic relative ('who' / 'that') used where modern Persian would have که; بود is past tense 'was'. شهریار زمین is a genitival phrase 'the sovereign/king of the land' (شهریار = king; زمین = land/earth). Together this is a relative nominal phrase 'one who was king of the earth.'
segments.s036-g118: نه negates the following predicate. بازی‌ست is بازی + است ('is a play' / 'is trivial'); here negated it means 'it is no mere game.' با او literally 'with him' indicates the target/participant of the action. سگالید is the noun/infinitival/poetic form from سگالیدن 'to set dogs on / to harry with dogs' (used here as the action in question). کین 'hatred/vengeance' qualifies سگالید (i.e., setting dogs on him out of enmity). The phrase therefore says: 'it is no play to set dogs on him in hatred.'
segments.s036-g119: شنیدستم is an archaic/poetic perfect/past first‑person 'I heard' / 'I was told.' از مردم راه‌جوی is a prepositional phrase 'from people who travel' (از = from; مردم = people; راه‌جوی = seekers of the road = travelers/wayfarers). Together: 'I have heard from traveling folk.'
segments.s036-g120: که introduces an indirect question/relative clause ('that / what'). ضحاک را uses the object/topic marker را to mark Zahhak as the affected person. ز (here part of زو) is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' and the following token و in the manuscript functions as the reduced/clitic pronoun (in practice 'او' = 'him') — together z+o = 'from him / because of him'. چه آمد means 'what came' or 'what befell'; بروی is a poetic verb form meaning 'upon him / befell' in this context. The clause as a whole: 'what befell Zahhak because of him.'

sentences.s036-s21: She came prancing up close to the cypress, just as one does before a flower when scattering.
sentences.s036-s22: He kissed the ground and displayed his fatness; upon that he added a lesser praise.
sentences.s036-s23: To Jandal thus the king of Yemen said: “May your mouth never be without praise.”
sentences.s036-s24: What message do you have? What command do you give — if you are indeed an honored messenger on the road?
sentences.s036-s1: When his year had advanced by a single hand-span, three honoured children came forth.
sentences.s036-s2: By the fortune of the world‑ruler, all three sons were of royal stock — three princes from the gateway of the golden crown.
sentences.s036-s3: In stature like a cypress and in face like the spring, in every respect resembling a sovereign.
sentences.s036-s4: Of these three, two were chaste — from Shahrnāz; the remaining one, the younger, was of fair‑faced Arnawāz.
sentences.s036-s5: The inexperienced father, untried in pampering and fame, set his step before the elephants.
sentences.s036-s6: Fereydun called one of those renowned men of his the more precious before (him).
sentences.s036-s7: How great was his renown—like a crag full of skill; in every affair a compassionate helper to the king.
sentences.s036-s8: He said to him, “Go about the world and choose three daughters from the noble line.”
sentences.s036-s65: Speaking and striving are our custom; flashing the reins and the spear is our faith.
sentences.s036-s66: With the dagger we shall seize the earth; with the spear we shall seize the air.
sentences.s036-s67: If you have three noble children, reveal your head like the full moon and close your lips.
sentences.s036-s68: But if you seek a remedy for the affair, you will fear this kingship.
sentences.s036-s25: Jandal said to him: “You always keep joy far from yourself and keep harm away from your hand.”
sentences.s036-s26: From Iran a certain younger man, like a shaman, has brought a message to the king of Yemen.
sentences.s036-s27: I give greetings to Fereydun the Fortunate; whatever speech they ask, I will reply.
sentences.s036-s28: Praise to you from Fereydun's circle; great is the one who does not possess wisdom.
sentences.s036-s9: Three sisters of one mother and one father — fairy-faced, pure, and jewel-like fit for a king.
sentences.s036-s10: As a fitting recompense for my three children, such as befits their bond with me.
sentences.s036-s11: In rank and in appearance the three are one; few of them are unaware of this.
sentences.s036-s12: When the band heard Khosrow's words, it devised a single pure counsel.
sentences.s036-s13: who had an alert heart and a pure mind, a smooth/flattering tongue and a fitting, pithy way with words.
sentences.s036-s14: He left the presence of the spahbed and set out on the road, somewhat reluctant, with a few men who were his well‑wishers.
sentences.s036-s15: One by one he summoned the chiefs of Iran into his presence, questioned them, and heard every sort of report.
sentences.s036-s16: In every realm that was foremost in the world there was a maiden kept behind the curtain.
sentences.s036-s61: On this matter, whatever words you have remembered, tell them all — you must lay them completely open to me.
sentences.s036-s62: The world‑tested, brave chiefs opened (their mouths) one by one to answer with their tongues.
sentences.s036-s63: For we, his equals, do not regard that as counsel — whenever any breeze blows, you are moved from your place.
sentences.s036-s64: If Fereydun became king of the world, we are not servants to be hung with earrings.
sentences.s036-s33: Free of every desire and every wish, their hand reaches out toward every longing.
sentences.s036-s34: I hid these three precious ones inside (in secrecy); now three pairs of princes are required.
sentences.s036-s35: From the affair I suddenly obtained news; at this tidings I hastened swiftly.
sentences.s036-s36: Where, behind the veil, do you keep the faces of the three chaste ones, O seeker of a name?
sentences.s036-s41: Fereydun gave me this message: 'You, answer whatever comes to your mind.'
sentences.s036-s42: When the king of Yemen heard his message, he withered like jasmine torn up by the Zab.
sentences.s036-s43: He kept saying at my bedside that my world-wise one would not live to see three months.
sentences.s036-s44: I had a clear day, but at the renewed night my two lips must open to answer.
sentences.s036-s37: When I heard the fresh, unspoken name of Maran — all three — my heart became joyful.
sentences.s036-s38: For we, too, did not recall the name of the three fortunate-born when it came to light.
sentences.s036-s39: Now these two kinds of precious gems must be mingled with one another.
sentences.s036-s40: The three veiled faces and the three rural seekers are worthy of their due without debate.
sentences.s036-s69: Do not seek from him weighty (substantial) desires whose deeds no face may see.
sentences.s036-s70: When the renowned heard that talk, neither the high nor the low in the world had seen it.
sentences.s036-s53: He was about to be parted from my sight; we must devise a plan with you.
sentences.s036-s54: The messenger said: 'The king said thus — that we have three kings, fair of aspect.'
sentences.s036-s55: Whoever of the three turns to my bond — my son, concealed with three faces.
sentences.s036-s56: If I say 'yes' while my heart is empty of that, my lie would not be acceptable to the moon‑like (one).
sentences.s036-s17: You searched out all their hidden secrets; you heard all their names and voices.
sentences.s036-s18: He saw no one among the wealthy landholders who could ever be worthy of Afrīdūn.
sentences.s036-s19: Wise, clear‑hearted and pure‑bodied, he came up to the cypress of the king of Yemen.
sentences.s036-s20: At the crag he found a sign that fitted (me and) her exactly; he sought three daughters, just as Fereydun had searched.
sentences.s036-s29: He told me, “Tell the king of Yemen that when the hour comes he should smell the musk.”
sentences.s036-s30: Know this, O chief resource of the Arabs: that from an ill/star of evil you (are) ever‑lasting and unharmed.
sentences.s036-s31: I have a flourishing kingship — that very treasure, manly virtue, and the strength of my hand.
sentences.s036-s32: Three sons worthy of the crown and the occasion; if in the tale there were a time, (it would be) the moon’s hour.
sentences.s036-s45: He said to the poet, "O Namjuy, one must mind the proper time in such a conversation."
sentences.s036-s46: Do not be hasty in answering now; I have a few secrets with a guide.
sentences.s036-s47: He quickly chose a place for the messenger; then he looked into the inner affairs.
sentences.s036-s48: He came into the court, made his presentation and shut (it); many thoughtful men were seated in the assembly.
sentences.s036-s49: He summoned many spearmen from the plain — the proven leaders — to his side.
sentences.s036-s50: They brought forth the hidden from concealment; they told all the secrets before them.
sentences.s036-s51: That, for us in the world, by the bond of his kin, three candles shine brightly before our sight.
sentences.s036-s52: Fereydun sent a message to me; before me he spread a skilful trap.
sentences.s036-s57: And if I were to entrust my desires to you, my heart would become full of fire and my face full of water (tears).
sentences.s036-s58: And if I turn my head away from his command, and if I incline aside from his covenant.
sentences.s036-s59: One who was the sovereign of the earth — it is no mere game to set dogs upon him in hatred.
sentences.s036-s60: I heard from wayfaring people what came upon Zahhak because of him.
